title: How to Win a Space War | Andreessen Horowitz
gist: a16z's American Dynamism partners argue orbit is now an active warfighting domain where the U.S. holds a commanding but unsafe lead, and that winning requires maximizing upmass, scaling commercial satellite manufacturing, and proliferating disaggregated military constellations rather than relying on a few exquisite, vulnerable satellites. The thesis maps directly onto a16z's portfolio bets in launch, ground stations, and satellite manufacturing.

insights.claim: The ability to put mass into orbit ('upmass') is the single most decisive advantage in a space war, because launch is physically hard and a leader can 'spend' that mass on shielding, propellant, redundancy, or proliferated constellations however it wants.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Treat orbital launch capacity (rockets, pads, supply chains) as the Tier 1 chokepoint; companies that scale upmass are the foundational layer of the space-defense stack.
insights.quote: upmass is everything. It's hard to get mass into orbit, so if you can do it better than your adversaries, you have a massive advantage.
insights.locator: The implications for space warfighting

insights.claim: Only 14 organizations worldwide successfully launched multiple orbital-class rockets in 2025 (four U.S., six China, four rest-of-world), and there are only about 40 active rocket launch pads globally, making launch infrastructure a concentrated, attackable bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Launch is a scarce, defensible asset; control of pads and factories effectively controls access to the space domain.
insights.locator: Only 14 organizations in the entire world

insights.claim: In 2025 the U.S. launched about 9.3 tons to orbit per day versus China's 1.8 and Russia's 0.4, but a decade ago the U.S. led with only 0.9 tons/day, meaning the U.S. 10x-ed while China 4.5x-ed and Russia stagnated.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: The U.S. lead is real but not safe; watch China's launch cadence growth as the key competitive indicator and back next-gen heavy launchers to maintain the gap.
insights.quote: Russia stagnated, China 4.5x-ed, and we 10x-ed. Standing still is not an option if we want to maintain our launch advantage.
insights.locator: In 2025, the United States launched about 9.3 tons

insights.claim: SpaceX's Falcon 9 is the only launch vehicle ever built that delivers mass to orbit repeatedly, reliably, and cost-effectively, with reusability as its secret ingredient, and SpaceX remains the only entity worldwide to land orbital-class boosters at scale more than 10 years after first doing so in 2015.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: SpaceX's reusability moat is still unmatched a decade on; competitors closing this gap (Blue Origin, Rocket Lab) are the key new entrants to track.
insights.quote: humanity has only invented one launch vehicle capable of delivering mass to orbit repeatedly, reliably, and cost-effectively: SpaceX's Falcon 9 rocket.
insights.locator: Every part of the rocket supply chain is hard

insights.claim: Blue Origin landed and reflew a New Glenn booster (Nov 2025 / Apr 2026) but suffered second-stage failures and in May 2026 had its 'worst disaster in company history' when a New Glenn exploded during a test-fire and destroyed its orbital/lunar launch pad, just as it prepared to scale to 100 launches per year.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Blue Origin's path to challenging SpaceX has been set back materially; single-pad dependence is a strategic fragility for any launch provider.
insights.locator: Blue Origin, the American rocket company owned by Jeff Bezos

insights.claim: Rocket Lab has achieved 46 consecutive successful Electron launches over 2.5 years but Electron is small (320 kg to LEO) and non-reusable; its Falcon-9-class reusable Neutron has not yet launched and slipped to Q4 2026.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Neutron is the most credible near-term medium-lift reusable challenger to Falcon 9; its first launch is a key catalyst to watch.
insights.locator: Rocket Lab, a New Zealand-founded

insights.claim: China's launch industry is the largest outside the U.S., with state Long March programs (CASC/SAST/CALT) plus five well-funded 'space dragon' private firms (CAS Space, LandSpace, Galactic Energy, iSpace, Space Pioneer), and is unhindered by environmental reviews, though no Chinese entity has recovered an orbital booster yet.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: China's launch buildout is fast and regulation-light; first Chinese reusable booster recovery would be a major competitive milestone to monitor.
insights.locator: China today has by far the largest

insights.claim: All U.S. spaceports are located on the water because the FAA refuses overland flight paths, making them relatively easy to attack by air, sea, or subsea in wartime; the authors argue the U.S. needs an inland launchpad and a fleet of launch sites across the sparsely populated American West.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Spaceport resilience and inland sites are an underinvested gap; regulatory change here could unlock new launch infrastructure investment.
insights.locator: This means in practice that all of our spaceports

themes.theme: Physics of space as a warfighting domain

insights.claim: Straight-line distance is nearly meaningless in space because delta-v governs movement: reaching a 350 km circular orbit takes 9.4 km/s, and another 9.4 km/s lands you on Mars, so a satellite on Mars is 'just as close' to Low Earth Orbit as one on Earth.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Evaluate space assets and weapons by their delta-v budget, not geography; propulsion capacity is the true currency of orbital mobility.
insights.quote: If you can get your ship into orbit, you're halfway to anywhere.
insights.locator: Delta-v is counterintuitive

insights.claim: Satellites are inherently fragile, immobile 'big, fat, juicy targets' that generally cannot survive a direct strike of any kind, forcing a design tradeoff between one bulletproof exquisite satellite and many attritable normal ones.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: The economics favor attritable, proliferated assets over exquisite ones; satellite-manufacturing-at-scale players benefit from this shift.
insights.locator: Practically, this means that satellites tend to be fragile

insights.claim: Attribution in space is extremely hard, giving space a 'thicker fog of war than any other domain': a Starlink satellite that recently broke apart required a detailed investigation to attribute the cause to an internal energetic source rather than foul play.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Space domain awareness, telescopes, and forensic-attribution capabilities are critical and under-built; an opening for new defense-tech entrants.
insights.quote: space has a thicker fog of war than any other domain.
insights.locator: First, attribution in space is extremely hard

insights.claim: Space is physically massive but operationally tiny: geostationary orbit is effectively a single 1-dimensional line (one altitude, one latitude) with only ~720 km average spacing between satellites, and LEO altitudes function like GEO longitudes due to circular-orbit coordination needs.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Orbital 'real estate' (key altitudes, GEO slots, Lagrange points) is scarce and grabbable; first movers and incumbents holding slots have durable positional value.
insights.quote: space is operationally small despite being physically large.
insights.locator: #3: Space is physically massive, but operationally small

insights.claim: Space is a fragile domain where a single kinetic strike spawns thousands of diverging debris orbits at the same altitude, and a malicious actor could 'hit the reset button' on a dense orbital shell, with a nuclear detonation potentially destroying 93% of all satellites across all orbits per a preliminary UC Berkeley estimate.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Fragility is the defining constraint of space warfare; debris-free 'reversible' weapons and protect-and-defend capabilities are the strategically viable categories.
insights.quote: a single nuke could destroy 93% of all satellites, across all orbits.
insights.locator: Fourth, and by far most importantly, space is a fragile domain

insights.claim: Space-to-ground weapons such as kinetic 'rods from God' are inevitable but require so much delta-v off Earth's surface that they will only make economic sense once manufactured in orbit, e.g. by a lunar colony flinging rocks down the gravity well.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Orbital and lunar in-situ manufacturing is the precondition for space-to-ground weapons; a long-dated frontier, not a near-term investment.
insights.locator: Third, space-to-ground weapons are inevitable

insights.claim: SpaceX has launched over 12,000 Starlink satellites (8,600 active) and is only a few hundred short of matching every other satellite ever launched worldwide combined, while the next nine largest constellations have under 1,600 active satellites combined.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Starlink's dominance is a category of one; the gap signals huge whitespace for sovereign and rival constellations and for satellite-manufacturing capacity.
insights.locator: Most objects in orbit today are Starlink satellites

insights.claim: A reusable orbital-class launcher was a necessary precondition for any successful LEO megaconstellation, as proven by the failures of pre-Starlink players: Iridium ($5B), Globalstar ($4B), ICO Global ($2.6B), Teledesic ($1B), and OneWeb (bankrupt after $3.4B) all incinerated cash.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Don't back LEO constellations without cheap reusable launch access; the unit economics only close on the back of low-cost upmass.
insights.quote: having a reusable, orbital-class launcher was a necessary precondition for a successful satellite megaconstellation.
insights.locator: LEO has always had appeal

insights.claim: Starlink generated $11 billion in revenue in 2025 with an average of 6,000 satellites, comparable to DirecTV's ~$10.3 billion (2026 dollars) in 2001 with just five GEO satellites, illustrating LEO's vastly higher capital intensity per dollar of revenue.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: LEO constellations trade massive capex/satellite count for latency and coverage; evaluate against GEO incumbents on capital efficiency, not just revenue.
insights.locator: In 2001, DirecTV generated about $10.3 billion

insights.claim: Earth-observation revenue follows a 50/25/25 rule (50% U.S. government, 25% allied governments, 25% commercial), meaning satellite imaging remains a defense-dominated market despite commercial entrants like Planet Labs.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Imaging businesses live or die on government contracts; commercial-only demand is still too thin to anchor a standalone thesis.
insights.locator: the rule of thumb in the US industry is that 50%

insights.claim: Nations with leading private space firms can build exclusive government systems like SpaceX's Starshield (a Starlink-like constellation built by SpaceX, operated by the U.S. Government), and satellite factories from Apex, K2, Endurosat, and Impulse Space provide the modern equivalent of Ford's WWII Willow Run bomber plant for wartime surge capacity.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Domestic satellite-manufacturing capacity is a strategic dual-use asset; satellite-bus and component factories are a direct play on wartime depth.
insights.locator: Similarly, nations with leading private space enterprises

insights.claim: What matters for deterrence is not absolute dollars on orbit but the relative value of orbit to each nation: Russia has just over 300 satellites versus ~1,200 for China and ~11,500 for the U.S. (Iran has 14), so Russia and Iran have far more to gain from an orbital 'reset', making Russia's nuclear threat more credible.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Low-stakes adversaries are the most dangerous in space; the U.S.'s growing orbital dependence is itself a vulnerability that raises the value of resilient architectures.
insights.quote: the relative value of orbit to that nation versus its adversaries.
insights.locator: But relying on that deterrent effect is risky

themes.theme: Resilient military architecture: disaggregate and proliferate

insights.claim: The U.S. military operates dangerously few exquisite satellites — only ten workhorse satcom satellites, ten missile-warning satellites, and 32 GPS satellites — so degrading even a few would create prominent, predictable coverage holes an adversary could exploit.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Concentration risk in legacy U.S. military constellations is acute; the structural shift toward proliferated architectures is a durable demand driver.
insights.locator: America has only ten workhorse satcom satellites

insights.claim: Resilience requires five disciplines per Space Force Gen. Saltzman — disaggregation (one mission per satellite), diversification (multi-orbit hybrid architectures), distribution/proliferation (many small assets), maneuverability ('maneuver without regret'), and protection — which collectively shift the attack/defense math toward the defender.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Use these five axes to evaluate space-defense programs and startups; proliferated, single-mission, maneuverable systems are the favored architecture.
insights.locator: which — per Space Force General Chance Saltzman

insights.claim: Proliferated military architectures demand satellite autonomy, so capabilities like orbital 'dogfighting' and autonomous threat reaction to attacks or debris will become more prevalent.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: On-orbit autonomy software is a rising investment category as constellation counts explode beyond manual operability.
insights.locator: Finally, it is worth noting here that a distributed

insights.claim: Maneuverability requires new propulsion: modern electric propulsion is too low-thrust while storable chemical propulsion burns propellant too fast, so a high-thrust, high-specific-impulse thruster remains an unsolved, science-fiction-level technology gap.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Breakthrough in-space propulsion is a high-value open problem; whoever cracks high-thrust efficient propulsion wins orbital 'dogfights' which are decided by delta-v.
insights.locator: Maneuverability

insights.claim: Many nations have demonstrated kinetic anti-satellite capability — the Secure World Foundation logs 33 U.S. tests (ending 2008), 26 Russian (most recent 2021), 14 Chinese (most recent 2023), and 2 Indian (both 2019) — and the DIA believes China can now strike targets possibly all the way out to GEO.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: ASAT capability is widespread and China's reach is expanding to high orbits; no orbit is a safe haven, raising demand for protect-and-defend assets.
insights.locator: The Secure World Foundation maintains a comprehensive catalog

themes.theme: The cold war: grey-zone tactics, spectrum, and ground systems

insights.claim: Space war today is best modeled as a cold war fought with hard-to-attribute grey-zone tactics — RF jamming/spoofing, satellite-blinding lasers ('dazzling'), and cyberattacks — that deny but do not destroy assets and avoid generating debris.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: The active market today is electronic/cyber/laser counterspace, not kinetic; non-kinetic offense and defense are where near-term demand and conflict actually sit.
insights.quote: modern space war is full of grey-zone tactics that are either hard to attribute or hard to classify as outright acts of war.
insights.locator: Perhaps the simplest way to understand

insights.claim: Ground systems, spectrum, cybersecurity, and regulation are chronically neglected because 'satellites are sexier than support systems' — exemplified by Raytheon's GPS OCX ground program, kicked off in 2010 for $3.7B by 2016 but cancelled 16 years and $6.3B later, stranding the working M-Code waveform already on orbit.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Ground-segment, spectrum, and software are underfunded chokepoints; modern ground-station and software players (e.g. Northwood Space) address a real gap.
insights.quote: satellites are sexier than support systems
insights.locator: As a case in point, consider Raytheon's

insights.claim: Spectrum is a controllable bottleneck and a valuable asset: legacy bands (S, C, Ku) are contested and licenses extremely valuable, evidenced by Amazon reportedly trying to buy a satellite company for $11.5 billion presumably more for spectrum than satellites.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Spectrum licenses are a scarce, denial-prone, high-value asset; valuations of satellite firms should weight spectrum holdings heavily.
insights.locator: Amazon, for instance, is trying to buy a satellite company

insights.claim: China is waging a regulatory cold war at the ITU, accused of registering fake 'paper satellites' to block law-abiding nations from key orbits, having held the ITU presidency for years and set to host the 2027 World Radiocommunication Conference, exploiting that the Radio Regs have 'immense influence and no teeth.'
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Regulatory/lawfare capture is a real competitive front; orbital-slot and spectrum positioning depends on ITU dynamics the U.S. is losing.
insights.locator: a far less commonly discussed issue

insights.claim: Under Article 1 of the Outer Space Treaty no nation can claim orbital territory or lawfully keep others away from its satellites, so Russia surrounding ICEYE at 500 meters or tailing an NRO satellite may be reckless but not illegal, and treaties historically mean little once conflict goes hot.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Legal norms offer little protection for orbital assets; deterrence must come from capability, not treaties — favoring defense-tech over diplomacy as the operative lever.
insights.locator: The lines are blurry between military and commercial

insights.claim: A nuclear detonation in orbit is catastrophic and not contained to Russia: the 1962 Starfish Prime test created an artificial radiation belt that killed over a third of then-operational satellites, and all nine nuclear nations can loft weapons to orbital altitude, some with so little space infrastructure they risk no friendly fire.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Nuclear ASAT is an existential tail risk for all space assets; resilience and missile-intercept (e.g. Golden Dome-type) capabilities are the only mitigations.
insights.locator: On July 9, 1962, the United States detonated

insights.claim: Winning a space war requires two-fold capability: defensive technologies to intercept ASAT missiles (active denial like Golden Dome, left-of-launch ops, dodge propulsion, protect-and-defend satellites) and publicly-acknowledged offensive capabilities so overwhelming that no adversary tests U.S. resolve.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Both orbital missile defense and credible debris-free offense are needed; these are the explicit demand categories a16z is signaling it will fund.
insights.locator: This means that the capabilities you need to defend the domain are two-fold

insights.claim: Viable offensive space weapons must avoid cascading debris, taking forms like directed-energy weapons against sensors, concentrated RF against receivers, and reversible/irreversible close-proximity effects (robotic manipulation, chemicals, low-collateral kinetics), with the hard constraint being the attacker-defender propulsion math.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Directed-energy and proximity/rendezvous capabilities are the technically and politically feasible offense categories; delta-v advantage is the deciding factor.
insights.locator: These offensive technologies will take many forms

insights.claim: The central strategic lesson is that fragility and weakness invite coercion while resilience and strength deter it, so if the U.S. can launch at unmatched scale, manufacture satellites at volume, distribute capability across thousands of assets and multiple orbits, win the cold war, and credibly defend the domain without destroying it, it wins.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: The full investment thesis: launch + manufacturing + proliferation + cold-war dominance + non-destructive defense is the winning stack to back across the space-defense sector.
insights.quote: fragility and weakness invite coercion; resilience and strength deter it.
insights.locator: This is the central lesson of modern space warfighting

themes.theme: Active orbital conflict: present-day threat assessment

insights.claim: In May 2026 Russia mobilized five satellites to surround Finland's ICEYE-X36 imaging satellite (which sells imagery to Ukraine), burning significant fuel to match its 550 km orbit with a closest pass of 500 meters, an act consistent with official Russian policy that commercial infrastructure aiding military efforts is a legitimate retaliatory target.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Commercial satellites are now active targets; dual-use commercial space firms carry geopolitical risk that should be priced into the sector.
insights.locator: Russia just mobilized a fleet of satellites

insights.claim: All three space superpowers are actively at war in orbit today: Russia is jamming GPS across Eastern Europe and developing a nuclear ASAT capability (confirmed by the White House in Feb 2024), China has contemplated destroying Starlink and is racing to seize lunar south pole water ice, and Russian/Chinese systems interfere with U.S. military satellites 'every single day' per Gen. David Thompson.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Orbital conflict is current, not hypothetical; the threat environment underpins sustained government demand for counterspace and resilient systems.
insights.quote: interfering with military satellites "every single day."
insights.locator: As we discussed earlier, the United States, Russia, and China are actively at war

insights.claim: China is building counterspace capabilities at 'breathtaking pace' to deny U.S. and allied access on demand, per Gen. Stephen Whiting's 2025 Senate testimony, and has demonstrated dogfighting maneuvers (2024), an EW satellite (2025), and hauled a dead satellite out of GEO (2022).
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: China's counterspace progress is the central long-term competitive risk; track its maneuver, EW, and orbital-servicing demonstrations as leading indicators.
insights.locator: And in 2025, Gen. Stephen Whiting told the Senate

insights.claim: Sovereignty is now at a premium with every space-faring nation building independent military infrastructure — separate navigation systems (GPS, GLONASS, BeiDou, Galileo, NAVIC, QZSS), weather, missile-warning, and space stations — partly because reliance on shared assets can be punished, as when Starlink allegedly turned off over Ukraine in 2022.
insights.speaker: Christian Keil, Alex Oliver
insights.so_what: Sovereign space capability is a durable global spending theme; expect every capable nation to fund domestic launch, PNT, and constellations.
insights.locator: That there exists a premium on sovereignty
